data.title: Ford, Lear, Visteon downgraded at Goldman Sachs on tariff risks, weaker demand
data.description:
Goldman Sachs analysts on Thursday downgraded their outlook for the U.S. automotive and industrial technology sectors, citing growing concerns over global competition, weakening consumer demand and rising costs from tariffs.

The bank issued multiple rating changes and estimate revisions across the space, reflecting what it called a “more difficult cyclical dynamic” for the remainder of 2025 and beyond.

Ford downgraded on tariff exposure, weaker margins

Among the most notable changes, Ford Motor (NYSE:F) was cut to Neutral from Buy. Goldman acknowledged that its earlier bullish stance, initiated in September 2024, was overly optimistic.

Since then, the Wall Street consensus estimate for 2025 earnings per share has been revised down by 32% and the stock is down 10%, the analysts wrote, noting that the broader sector has fared even worse. Ford’s (NYSE:F) relative resilience was attributed to investor sentiment at the time of the previous upgrade and cost-cutting progress.

Still, the analysts flagged growing global competition, reduced consumer appetite and higher tariff burdens as key risks. While Ford’s (F) U.S. manufacturing footprint may help buffer against some tariff fallout, Goldman said valuation expansion compared with traditional peers and a slower-than-expected ramp in software and services are reasons for concern.

GM maintains Buy; Tesla and Rivian still Neutral

Goldman is sticking with its Buy rating on General Motors (NYSE:GM), citing its more attractive valuation versus Ford (F) and slightly lower expected tariff-related EPS impact. Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) and Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN) remain Neutral-rated, with the bank weighing risks from consumer softness and policy uncertainty against long-term opportunities in AI and vehicle architecture licensing, respectively.

Tier-1 suppliers hit hard: Lear, Visteon downgraded

The bank also downgraded auto suppliers Lear (NYSE:LEA) and Visteon (NASDAQ:VC) to Neutral from Buy, pointing to the sector's inability to fully offset lower industry volumes. Both companies face among the highest exposure to tariffs as a percentage of earnings before interest and taxes, which could significantly compress margins.

Goldman maintained Sell ratings on other suppliers with downside risk or structural headwinds, including Magna (NYSE:MGA), Luminar (NASDAQ:LAZR), QuantumScape (NYSE:QS) and ChargePoint (NYSE:CHPT).

Selective bright spots: BorgWarner, Sensata, Aurora

Despite the broader pessimism, Goldman upgraded a few select names:

  • BorgWarner (NYSE:BWA) was raised to Buy from Neutral, with analysts pointing to its favorable China exposure, in-region manufacturing strategy, and potential growth across all powertrain types, especially in EV content and hybrid solutions.
  • Sensata Technologies (NYSE:ST) was also upgraded to Buy, following a sharp 24% year-to-date selloff. Goldman called current valuation levels a trough, even on below-consensus earnings, and sees a path to EPS growth in 2026 and 2027 as industrial demand recovers and vehicle electrification gains pace.
  • Aurora Innovation (NASDAQ:AUR) received a bump to Neutral from Sell, with analysts noting that their previous bearish catalyst — slower autonomous trucking ramp — has largely played out. Aurora (NASDAQ:AUR) stock has surged 99% since being placed on the Sell list in January, despite revenue downgrades. Goldman now sees a scarcity premium and long-term investor interest in autonomy as supporting the stock.

Aurora is seen as a first-mover in autonomous trucking and is expected to begin driverless commercial operations this year, albeit on a small scale, the analysts wrote, while cautioning that the company still faces a multiyear path to profitability and will likely need additional capital.

Outlook: Tariff drag, valuation pressures

Goldman’s reset on the industry reflects deepening concerns across the U.S. auto complex, especially as new tariffs add cost pressures and consumer sentiment deteriorates. The bank's analysts suggest investors should expect ongoing estimate cuts and tighter margins, but also see selective opportunities emerging in names with tariff insulation, flexible cost structures and clear long-term positioning.

data.title: This is how the mega-caps are doing a week after the big selloffs
data.description:
Five of the mega-cap stocks have now turned green over the last week.

From the last five days, Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) is up 7.59%; followed by Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), up 3.54%; Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), up 2.19%, and Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL), up 1.06%.

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) is down 11.15% over the last five days; Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) is down 2.73%; Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B) is down 3.03%, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) is down 2.5%; and Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) is down 0.32%.

However, all of these stocks – aside from Berkshire (NYSE:BRK.B) – are below their 50-day moving average. The multinational conglomerate is up 4.35% from its 50-day moving average.

Also, all these are rated “neutral” by Bespoke Investment Group, aside from Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), which is rated “oversold.”

“There is likely more froth to be wrung out of the mega caps,” said Charles Schwab Chief Investment Strategist Liz Ann Sonders last week. “Unfortunately, given their weight in the cap-weighted indexes, any continued weakness would put disproportionate pressure on the market."

data.title: Occidental Petroleum expects higher prices for oil and gas production in Q1
data.description:
Occidental Petroleum (NYSE:OXY) said late Wednesday the prices it received for crude oil and natural gas production in Q1 were higher than in the previous quarter, according to an SEC filing, a week after Exxon Mobil also signaled that higher oil and gas prices could boost its Q1 earnings.

Occidental (NYSE:OXY) said its total worldwide average realized price for oil output was $71.07/bbl, compared to $69.73/bbl it realized in Q4 2024, its average realized price for total natural gas production rose to $2.30/Mcf from $1.26/Mcf ing the prior quarter, and its realized price for natural gas liquids rose 19% Q/Q to $25.94/bbl.

Benchmark Brent crude prices averaged $74.98/bbl in Q1, up 1.3% from the prior quarter, while U.S. natural gas prices jumped 30% Q/Q to $3.62/MMBtu.

Roth MKM analyst Leo Mariani said Occidental's (NYSE:OXY) natural gas realization for the quarter was nearly 7% above his expectations, while NGL prices topped estimates by 8.5%.

Occidental (OXY) will report its Q1 financial results on May 8.

data.title: KC Fed's Schmid stays focused on inflation outlook amid risks to growth
data.description:
Kansas City Fed President Jeffrey Schmid said on Thursday he's keeping "squarely focused on the outlook for inflation,"  when faced with the prospect that the Federal Reserve "will have to balance inflation risks against growth and employment concerns."

Uncertainty over tariffs has made the economic outlook complicated, with upside risks to inflation increasing as downside risks to employment and growth build.

"Now, with renewed price pressures likely, I am not willing to take any chances when it comes to maintaining the Fed’s credibility on inflation," he said at the Structured Finance Network's Independent Finance Roundtable, according to prepared text of this speech.

"While in theory, tariffs may have only temporary effects on inflation (although persistent effects on the level of prices), I would be hesitant to take too much solace from theory in this environment. Given the recent experience with high inflation, I am concerned that any further jump in prices could further push up inflation expectations," he said.

He points out, though, "We are entering a challenging period from a position of strength. The economy has shown a lot of momentum and has repeatedly brushed off periodic recession fears in recent years."

Schmid also discussed the longer-term outlook for policy, noting that structural demographic shifts suggest that interest rates are likely to decline. However, the supply-demand balance of government debt suggests that interest rates may stay high. 

"Evidence suggests that countries with faster population growth and younger populations tend to have higher levels of capital investment and a greater desire to borrow, both of which work to push up interest rates," he said. "In contrast, older, slower growing economies borrow and invest less, pushing down interest rates."

The U.S. population is getting older. From 1980 to now, the U.S. workforce increased by 66M, but over the next 45 years, the U.S. Census Bureau projects that the working age population will only increase by 2M. That means productivity will be the sole determinant in economic growth.

On the debt side of the equation, the U.S. government deficit continues to increase, issuing the supply of government debt as foreign demand for U.S. debt may be diminishing. "Growing supply, in tandem with shrinking demand, is a recipe for higher interest rates in the longer-term," Schmid said.

data.title: Amazon's Andy Jassy clarifies that he never bid for TikTok U.S.
data.description:
Amazon's (NASDAQ:AMZN) top boss, Andy Jassy, said in an interview on CNBC Wednesday that he is not bidding for TikTok U.S.

The CEO of the e-commerce and tech giant did not comment on M&A rumors when asked further during the CNBC interview.

The statement by Jassy comes in direct contrast to several media reports from earlier this month that claimed that the company submitted a last-minute bid for the short video content app.

Jassy's clarification comes after U.S. President Donald Trump further extended the deadline for the app by 75 days to keep it operational against the sale-or-ban law taking effect, which was passed under the Biden administration.

A Beijing spokesperson said Wednesday that any deal for TikTok U.S. has to be in compliance with Chinese law. China also indicated it would not approve any deal under Trump's reciprocal tariff threat.

data.title: Trump’s 90-day tariff pause is not as helpful as it sounds, Citi says
data.description:
Wall Street roared back to life after President Trump announced a 90-day suspension of tariffs affecting more than 75 countries—news that resulted in a historic day of gains for markets.

But, despite the initial optimism, not everyone is convinced that the economic turbulence is over.

Citi Research cautioned that the reprieve might offer only short-term relief. “Pausing reciprocal tariffs excluding China does not mean the US economy has avoided a slowdown in growth and rise in inflation,” Citi said in a recent note to clients. 

The investment institution emphasized that the broader trade war remains very much alive. The baseline 10% tariff, combined with an additional 125% in levies on most Chinese goods and targeted industry tariffs, has pushed the average effective U.S. tariff rate up by roughly 21 percentage points since the start of the year. 

Citi added that trade-related uncertainty is far from over. With Chinese imports still heavily taxed, a wave of non-China imports could now flood in—potentially weighing on economic growth in the second quarter. 

As a result, Citi noted that it anticipates the FOMC will come in and cut interest rates in either May or June. 

S&P 500 Funds: (MUTF:FXAIX), (MUTF:VFIAX), (MUTF:VFFSX), (MUTF:SWPPX), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS) and (NYSEARCA:SPXU). 

China ETFs: (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:CQQQ), (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:GXC), (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:FLCH), (BATS:CNYA), (NYSEARCA:ASHR), and (NYSEARCA:YINN).

data.title: Charles Schwab launches new alternative investments platform
data.description:
• Charles Schwab (NYSE:SCHW) announced on Thursday the launch of a new alternative investments platform for eligible retail clients with over $5M in household assets.
  • The Schwab Alternative Investments Select expands on its wealth management capabilities.
  • The platform was initially rolled out to a small group beginning in October 2024, and is now available to all eligible retail clients.

data.title: GM downgraded as tariffs could cost the company $5B annually
data.description:
Accounting for the impact of tariffs on the company’s cost structure along with the impact to auto demand, UBS lowered its earnings estimates for General Motors (NYSE:GM) by as much as 33% and downgraded the stock to Neutral from Buy with a corresponding 20% cut to its target price.

Putting a price tag on tariffs, UBS’s Joseph Spak assumes vehicles produced in Mexico and Canada have ~50% U.S. content, placing a 25% tariff burden to half of the assumed $35K material cost per vehicle. This effectively raises the price of a GM (NYSE:GM) vehicle made in Mexico and China by $4.3K.

For vehicles made in China and Korea, Spak places a 25% tariff burden on the entire vehicle, or $6.25K per vehicle.

Without mitigation efforts, these assumptions will result in an annual cost headwind of ~$5B for the company, with the potential to increase if USMCA parts exemption is eliminated. This will not only increase the cost of the vehicle, but increase the need for pricing to offset costs, ultimately resulting in reduced volumes.

The net result leads Spak to low his 2025/26 auto EBIT estimates by 16%/27% to $10.1B/$7.5B, and total company EBIT by 13%/21% to $12.7B/$10.3B.

Profits will also be adversely impacted by the new tariff dynamic, with Spak’s 2025 earnings estimate lowered by 13% to $10.59 and 2026 estimate by 30% to $8.89. Assuming no buybacks in 2025 and 2026, Spak lowers his estimates for 2025 free cash flow by more than half to $5B and by 22% in 2026 to $4.7B.

After Wednesday’s respite to a four-day losing streak, GM (NYSE:GM) shares are back in negative turf, down 3% at Thursday’s open.

data.title: Syntec Optics secures $2.1M advanced optics order
data.description:
• Syntec Optics (NASDAQ:OPTX) on Thursday announced securing follow-on orders of $2.1 million for critical optics that integrate into the next-generation military scopes.
  • Syntec Optics previously announced the creation of a dedicated manufacturing cell at the facility and engineering efforts to scale for advanced optics.
  • Syntec's mission-critical optics are scheduled to be delivered through 2026.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow drop as tariff concerns still weigh, CPI comes in cooler
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages fell on Thursday as tariff worries lingered, while the CPI came in cooler than expected in March.

Early on and the S&P 500 (SP500) was -2.1%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -2.6%, and the Dow (DJI) was -1.7%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) gained 1 basis point to 4.34%. At the same time the 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 5 basis points to 3.85%.

The Consumer Price Index came in at -0.1% M/M vs. +0.1% consensus, +2.4% Y/Y vs. +2.6% consensus, and +2.8% prior.

Core CPI came in at +0.1% M/M vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.8% Y/Y vs. +3.0% consensus and +3.1% prior

It was nothing short of a historic day for markets on Wednesday, as U.S. President Donald Trump's pause on reciprocal tariffs against most countries except China sent Wall Street to its best day since the financial crisis of 2008. The S&P 500 rocketed +9.52%; the Nasdaq Composite exploded +12.16%, and the Dow surged +7.87% on Wednesday. 

"Market turmoil turned into elation yesterday as Trump announced a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for countries other than China, which marked a sizable backing off from last week’s tariff escalation," Deutsche Bank's Peter Sidorov said.

The European Union paused countermeasures on U.S. tariffs.

Trump on Wednesday said he would issue a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for more than 75 countries who had reached out to the U.S. for trade negotiations.

Perhaps most crucially, we are currently still on course for a disorderly economic decoupling between the world’s two largest economies, with no immediate signs of either the U.S. or China backing down, Sidorov said.

Trump also upped the tariff rate against China to 125%. 

Recession risk in the US is lower than yesterday but remains high, UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Initial jobless claims edged up to 223K in the past week.

The Fed balance sheet will also land on Thursday.
